infobox.value: (stylized in capital letters since 2025) is a [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels")-based, international biennial [arts festival](/wiki/Arts_festival "Arts festival") that highlights the [cultural heritage](/wiki/Cultural_heritage "Cultural heritage") and art scene of one invited country. On multiple occasions, the festival has also dedicated editions to a theme rather than a country. EUROPALIA was established with the aim of bringing the arts and culture of European countries to the heart of [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe") itself. Although, since 1989 – with Europalia Japan – non-european countries have also featured. The very first festival in 1969 was dedicated to Italy.
– *Rembrandt and his Time* (exhibition) at [Bozar](/wiki/Centre_for_Fine_Arts,_Brussels "Centre for Fine Arts, Brussels"), [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels") (Europalia Netherlands)
– *David Hockney* (exhibition) at [Bozar](/wiki/Centre_for_Fine_Arts,_Brussels "Centre for Fine Arts, Brussels"), [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels") (Europalia Great Britain)
– *Bruegel, a Dynasty of Painters* (exhibition) at [Bozar](/wiki/Centre_for_Fine_Arts,_Brussels "Centre for Fine Arts, Brussels"), [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels") (Europalia Belgium)

– *Venus Unveiled* (exhibition) at [Bozar](/wiki/Centre_for_Fine_Arts,_Brussels "Centre for Fine Arts, Brussels"), [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels") (Europalia Italia) – curator: [Umberto Eco](/wiki/Umberto_Eco "Umberto Eco")

– *The State of Things* (exhibition) at [Bozar](/wiki/Centre_for_Fine_Arts,_Brussels "Centre for Fine Arts, Brussels"), [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels") (Europalia China) – curators: [Ai Wei Wei](/wiki/Ai_Wei_Wei "Ai Wei Wei") and [Luc Tuymans](/wiki/Luc_Tuymans "Luc Tuymans")
– *Tropicália* (concert) by Tom Zé & Quintet at VIERNULVIER ([Ghent](/wiki/Ghent "Ghent")), DE SINGEL ([Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp "Antwerp")) and other venues (Europalia Brazil)
– *Power and Other Things* (exhibition) at [Bozar](/wiki/Centre_for_Fine_Arts,_Brussels "Centre for Fine Arts, Brussels"), [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels") (Europalia Indonesia)
– *Brancusi. Sublimation of Form* (exhibition) at [Bozar](/wiki/Centre_for_Fine_Arts,_Brussels "Centre for Fine Arts, Brussels"), [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels") (Europalia Romania)
– *La Ronde* by Boris Charmatz/Terrain (performance) at Brussels North Station (Europalia Trains & Tracks)
– Georgian Polyphony: Triple Bill Basiani Ensemble, Gori Women’s Choir & Georgian State Chamber Choir (concert) at Concertgebouw ([Bruges](/wiki/Bruges "Bruges")) and other venues (Europalia Georgia)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

| EUROPALIA | |
| --- | --- |
|  | |
| 2023 europalia georgia festival banner | |
| Genre | [Biennale](/wiki/Biennale "Biennale"); focuses on the cultural heritage and art scene of one invited country, and includes visual arts, performing arts, film, music, literature and debate |
| Frequency | Biennial |
| Locations | [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels"), [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium") |
| Inaugurated | 1969 |
| Website | [europalia.eu/en](https://europalia.eu/en) |

**EUROPALIA** (stylized in capital letters since 2025) is a [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels")-based, international biennial [arts festival](/wiki/Arts_festival "Arts festival") that highlights the [cultural heritage](/wiki/Cultural_heritage "Cultural heritage") and art scene of one invited country. On multiple occasions, the festival has also dedicated editions to a theme rather than a country. EUROPALIA was established with the aim of bringing the arts and culture of European countries to the heart of [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe") itself. Although, since 1989 – with Europalia Japan – non-european countries have also featured. The very first festival in 1969 was dedicated to Italy.

The name EUROPALIA is a combination of two words: "[Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe")" and "[Opalia](/wiki/Opalia "Opalia")", an [ancient Roman](/wiki/Ancient_Roman "Ancient Roman") [harvest festival](/wiki/Harvest_festival "Harvest festival") held in mid-December in honour of [Ops](/wiki/Ops "Ops"), earth-goddess and fertility deity. Her name lies at the root of the [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") word "Opus", that denotes a work of art.

For approximately four months (traditionally starting in October), and in partnership with a wide network of cultural partners (museums, theatre and concert halls and other venues), EUROPALIA presents a series of artistic and socio-cultural projects that bring together visual arts, performing arts, film, music, literature and debate. In recent years, newly commissioned projects and artistic residencies have started occupying a central place in the multidisciplinary programme, which focuses on both the heritage and the contemporary art scene of the invited country.

The opening event takes place in the capital, but the programme also unfolds in other cities across [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium"), such as [Liège](/wiki/Li%C3%A8ge "Liège") and [Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp "Antwerp"), among others. Other European cities (in countries such as [the Netherlands](/wiki/The_Netherlands "The Netherlands"), [France](/wiki/France "France"), [Luxembourg](/wiki/Luxembourg "Luxembourg") and [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany")) have also hosted europalia events.

The festival expenses are shared between Belgium and the invited country. EUROPALIA is financially supported by Belgium’s national, regional and community governments, among others.

The upcoming festival, starting in October 2025, will be EUROPALIA ESPAÑA.

## History of festivals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Europalia&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History of festivals")]

## Selection of notable events

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Europalia&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Selection of notable events")]

* **1971** – *Rembrandt and his Time* (exhibition) at [Bozar](/wiki/Centre_for_Fine_Arts,_Brussels "Centre for Fine Arts, Brussels"), [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels") (Europalia Netherlands)
* **1973** – *David Hockney* (exhibition) at [Bozar](/wiki/Centre_for_Fine_Arts,_Brussels "Centre for Fine Arts, Brussels"), [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels") (Europalia Great Britain)
* **1980** – *Bruegel, a Dynasty of Painters* (exhibition) at [Bozar](/wiki/Centre_for_Fine_Arts,_Brussels "Centre for Fine Arts, Brussels"), [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels") (Europalia Belgium)
* **2003** – *Venus Unveiled* (exhibition) at [Bozar](/wiki/Centre_for_Fine_Arts,_Brussels "Centre for Fine Arts, Brussels"), [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels") (Europalia Italia) – curator: [Umberto Eco](/wiki/Umberto_Eco "Umberto Eco")
* **2009** – *The State of Things* (exhibition) at [Bozar](/wiki/Centre_for_Fine_Arts,_Brussels "Centre for Fine Arts, Brussels"), [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels") (Europalia China) – curators: [Ai Wei Wei](/wiki/Ai_Wei_Wei "Ai Wei Wei") and [Luc Tuymans](/wiki/Luc_Tuymans "Luc Tuymans")
* **2011** – *Tropicália* (concert) by Tom Zé & Quintet at VIERNULVIER ([Ghent](/wiki/Ghent "Ghent")), DE SINGEL ([Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp "Antwerp")) and other venues (Europalia Brazil)
* **2017** – *Power and Other Things* (exhibition) at [Bozar](/wiki/Centre_for_Fine_Arts,_Brussels "Centre for Fine Arts, Brussels"), [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels") (Europalia Indonesia)
* **2019** – *Brancusi. Sublimation of Form* (exhibition) at [Bozar](/wiki/Centre_for_Fine_Arts,_Brussels "Centre for Fine Arts, Brussels"), [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels") (Europalia Romania)
* **2021** – *La Ronde* by Boris Charmatz/Terrain (performance) at Brussels North Station (Europalia Trains & Tracks)
* **2023** – Georgian Polyphony: Triple Bill Basiani Ensemble, Gori Women’s Choir & Georgian State Chamber Choir (concert) at Concertgebouw ([Bruges](/wiki/Bruges "Bruges")) and other venues (Europalia Georgia)

## General and Artistic Directors

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Europalia&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: General and Artistic Directors")]

* **1985 – 1993** Martine Baudin, General Director
* **1993 – 1996** Paul Vandenbussche, General Director
* **1996 – 2005** Luc Stainier, General Director
* **2005 – 2017** Kristine De Mulder, General Director
* **2014 – present** Dirk Vermaelen, Artistic Director
* **2017 – 2023** Koen Clement, General Director
* **2023 – present** Christian Salez, General Director
